title: Standardized Pre-clinical Surgical Animal Model Protocol for Ischemic Flap Healing
author: Aksamitiene, Heffelfinger, et al.
materials: Personal protective equipment (PPE): surgical gown, ear loop face mask, hair cap, shoe covers, sterile surgical gloves, non-sterile gloves, safety glasses.
Stainless steel instrument tray with flap cover – for autoclaving and storage of surgical instruments
Stainless steel Grafco Flat Type instrument tray – for placing the surgical items during the procedure (Additional File 2, Fig. S1)
Sharps disposal container – for disposal of needles and syringes
Biohazard disposal container and biohazard bags – for safe disposal of biohazardous trash, including pipette tips.
Single or dual-channel anesthesia system with gas vaporizer, inlet flowmeter, charcoal filters, high pressure oxygen hose, oxygen regulator, and low-profile anesthesia masks adapted for rodents.
Handheld hair clipper and/or trimmer (e.g., WAHL for Home Pet).
Isoflurane inhalant anesthetic for veterinary use (USP)

steps.title: Tissue Preparation for Histology
steps.instruction: Unisette™ 1-chamber histology embedding/processing cassettes with 1 mm openings (Ted Pella)—for smaller size and leaner specimens:Outside dimensions: 28.5 × 41 × 6.7 mm (1.12" × 1.61" × 0.26").Inside dimensions: 26 × 30 x 5 mm (1.02" × 1.18" × 0.2").Macrosette™ 1-chamber histology embedding/processing cassettes with 1 mm openings (Ted Pella)—for thicker specimensMicromesh™ 4-compartment biopsy cassettes – for excised pediclesCassette marking histology pen (e.g., Statmark by StatLab)90 ml containers with prefilled 10% Neutral Phosphate-Buffered Formalin (pH 7.2 ± 0.5; vials) (e.g., StatLab, # NB0345)1X PBS buffer without magnesium and calcium—for cassette washing after fixation. Store at RT.70% Ethanol solution – for cassette storage until analysis at + 4°C.Large glass staining dishes.Stains of interest, e.g., Masson’s Trichrome (MT) and Hematoxylin & Eosin (H&E).Classical histology lab equipment if not using Core Histology facility.

steps.title: Tissue Preparation for Histology
steps.instruction: Unisette™ 1-chamber histology embedding/processing cassettes with 1 mm openings (Ted Pella)—for smaller size and leaner specimens:Outside dimensions: 28.5 × 41 × 6.7 mm (1.12" × 1.61" × 0.26").Inside dimensions: 26 × 30 x 5 mm (1.02" × 1.18" × 0.2").

steps.title: Tissue Preparation for Histology
steps.instruction: Macrosette™ 1-chamber histology embedding/processing cassettes with 1 mm openings (Ted Pella)—for thicker specimens

steps.title: Tissue Preparation for Histology
steps.instruction: 90 ml containers with prefilled 10% Neutral Phosphate-Buffered Formalin (pH 7.2 ± 0.5; vials) (e.g., StatLab, # NB0345)

steps.title: Tissue Preparation for Histology
steps.instruction: 1X PBS buffer without magnesium and calcium—for cassette washing after fixation. Store at RT.

steps.title: Tissue Preparation for Histology
steps.instruction: 70% Ethanol solution – for cassette storage until analysis at + 4°C.

steps.title: Tissue Preparation for Histology
steps.instruction: Stains of interest, e.g., Masson’s Trichrome (MT) and Hematoxylin & Eosin (H&E).

steps.title: Tissue Preparation for Histology
steps.instruction: Classical histology lab equipment if not using Core Histology facility.

steps.title: Experimental Animal Preparation for Survival Surgery
steps.instruction: To reduce the time of animal exposure to anesthesia during the major survival surgery, and to minimize the risk of overlapping PI or SI time between the sequentially operated animals, the animals are shaved 16—24 h prior to flap raising procedure:The heating pad is set for 122°F (50 °C) and covered with two surgical underpads.The anesthesia machines is turned on to prefill the padded rodent sedation chamber with 4–5% induction isoflurane gas.The animal is placed in the chamber and kept there for up to 4 min.3% isoflurane flow is then redirected into the nose cone attached to the anesthesia machine.The sedated rat is removed from the chamber and placed onto the heating pad in a prone position.The lubricate ointment is generously applied onto animal’s eyes.The rat is placed back into a supine position and its fur is shaved until the nude skin is exposed.The top surgical pad containing shaved fur is removed.Shaved skin areas are wiped with betadine solution, which is copiously applied onto a non-woven sponge.Animal weight is recorded in the post-operative animal monitoring sheet (POAMS) (see Additional File 1).The animal is returned to the cage and monitored until awakened.

steps.title: Experimental Animal Preparation for Survival Surgery
steps.instruction: NOTE: If less than three consecutive animal surgeries are anticipated one after the other within the same day, animal shaving should be performed on the same day as major survival surgery without an animal being awakened. With experience and proper equipment, animal fur clipping takes less than 12 min. Chemical hair removal creams such as “Nair” are not recommended as they may affect both baseline and injury-inducible biological molecule distribution and/or expression patterns, cause skin irritation or allergic reaction.

steps.title: Experimental Animal Preparation for Survival Surgery
steps.instruction: The heating pad is set for 122°F (50 °C) and covered with two surgical underpads.

steps.title: Experimental Animal Preparation for Survival Surgery
steps.instruction: The anesthesia machines is turned on to prefill the padded rodent sedation chamber with 4–5% induction isoflurane gas.

steps.title: Experimental Animal Preparation for Survival Surgery
steps.instruction: The animal is placed in the chamber and kept there for up to 4 min.

steps.title: Experimental Animal Preparation for Survival Surgery
steps.instruction: 3% isoflurane flow is then redirected into the nose cone attached to the anesthesia machine.

steps.title: Experimental Animal Preparation for Survival Surgery
steps.instruction: The sedated rat is removed from the chamber and placed onto the heating pad in a prone position.

steps.title: Experimental Animal Preparation for Survival Surgery
steps.instruction: The lubricate ointment is generously applied onto animal’s eyes.

steps.title: Experimental Animal Preparation for Survival Surgery
steps.instruction: The rat is placed back into a supine position and its fur is shaved until the nude skin is exposed.

steps.title: Experimental Animal Preparation for Survival Surgery
steps.instruction: The top surgical pad containing shaved fur is removed.

steps.title: Experimental Animal Preparation for Survival Surgery
steps.instruction: Shaved skin areas are wiped with betadine solution, which is copiously applied onto a non-woven sponge.

steps.title: Experimental Animal Preparation for Survival Surgery
steps.instruction: Animal weight is recorded in the post-operative animal monitoring sheet (POAMS) (see Additional File 1).

steps.title: Experimental Animal Preparation for Survival Surgery
steps.instruction: The animal is returned to the cage and monitored until awakened.

steps.title: Preparation
steps.instruction: Appropriate PPE is donned by the surgeon.The HDPE tubes, 10% formalin-prefilled containers and histology cassettes are labeled for appropriate specimen.The vascular clamps, that are being stored in enzymatic solution, are removed, washed with distilled water, and then placed in an Eppendorf tube containing 70% alcohol.The hot glass bead sterilizer is turned on to preheat.The heating pad is set to reach 122°F (50 °C). It is covered with four surgical underpads: 1 extra-large and 3 medium or small.If an optional PVA sponge implantation is desired, then two sterile 9 mm × 15 mm ear wicks are placed onto 100 × 20 mm tissue culture dish prefilled with sterile PBS. These wicks are then cut into three pieces per each using a disposable scalpel. The lid of tissue dish is closed to prevent the PVA sponges from drying.The dose of analgesic agent is prepared for each individual animal based on the recorded animal weight (for example, our rats received 1.0–1.2 mg/kg of Buprenorphine-HCl-SR formulation subcutaneously (sc)).Dewar flask is prefilled with liquid nitrogen (LN2).Isoflurane and oxygen tank levels are checked, and the proper amount of isoflurane is added to the gas anesthesia machine chamber before the experiment if needed. The anesthesia machine is turned one to prefill the padded rat sedation chamber with 4–5% isoflurane gas.

steps.title: Preparation
steps.instruction: The HDPE tubes, 10% formalin-prefilled containers and histology cassettes are labeled for appropriate specimen.

steps.title: Preparation
steps.instruction: The vascular clamps, that are being stored in enzymatic solution, are removed, washed with distilled water, and then placed in an Eppendorf tube containing 70% alcohol.

steps.title: Preparation
steps.instruction: The hot glass bead sterilizer is turned on to preheat.

steps.title: Preparation
steps.instruction: The heating pad is set to reach 122°F (50 °C). It is covered with four surgical underpads: 1 extra-large and 3 medium or small.

steps.title: Preparation
steps.instruction: If an optional PVA sponge implantation is desired, then two sterile 9 mm × 15 mm ear wicks are placed onto 100 × 20 mm tissue culture dish prefilled with sterile PBS. These wicks are then cut into three pieces per each using a disposable scalpel. The lid of tissue dish is closed to prevent the PVA sponges from drying.

steps.title: Preparation
steps.instruction: The dose of analgesic agent is prepared for each individual animal based on the recorded animal weight (for example, our rats received 1.0–1.2 mg/kg of Buprenorphine-HCl-SR formulation subcutaneously (sc)).

steps.title: Preparation
steps.instruction: Isoflurane and oxygen tank levels are checked, and the proper amount of isoflurane is added to the gas anesthesia machine chamber before the experiment if needed. The anesthesia machine is turned one to prefill the padded rat sedation chamber with 4–5% isoflurane gas.

steps.title: Preparation for Procedure
steps.instruction: The HDPE tubes are labeled for appropriate specimen. A suggested label may contain a unique animal number, code for treatment, indication of left or right flap side (R/L), day of harvest and an abbreviated name of a harvested segment, e.g., “174,871, A, L7D”).10% buffered formalin container and histology cassettes are labeled for appropriate specimen.A rubber bucket is filled with ice pellets. If PVA sponges were implanted, then 2.0 mL Eppendorf tubes are filled with PVA sponge lysis or other buffer, depending on the type of subsequent wound exudate (WE) analysis, and placed on ice. Flat-tip tweezers are prepared to handle the sponges.Dewar flask is prefilled with LN2 for immediate tissue freezing. We suggest using curved HDPE tube grabbing scissors.CO2 levels are checked in the euthanasia cart. Isoflurane and oxygen tank levels are checked in the anesthesia cart.

steps.title: Preparation for Procedure
steps.instruction: The HDPE tubes are labeled for appropriate specimen. A suggested label may contain a unique animal number, code for treatment, indication of left or right flap side (R/L), day of harvest and an abbreviated name of a harvested segment, e.g., “174,871, A, L7D”).

steps.title: Preparation for Procedure
steps.instruction: 10% buffered formalin container and histology cassettes are labeled for appropriate specimen.

steps.title: Preparation for Procedure
steps.instruction: A rubber bucket is filled with ice pellets. If PVA sponges were implanted, then 2.0 mL Eppendorf tubes are filled with PVA sponge lysis or other buffer, depending on the type of subsequent wound exudate (WE) analysis, and placed on ice. Flat-tip tweezers are prepared to handle the sponges.

steps.title: Preparation for Procedure
steps.instruction: Dewar flask is prefilled with LN2 for immediate tissue freezing. We suggest using curved HDPE tube grabbing scissors.

steps.title: Preparation for Procedure
steps.instruction: CO2 levels are checked in the euthanasia cart. Isoflurane and oxygen tank levels are checked in the anesthesia cart.
